Just configured it this weekend on a new phone. Much easier. Wyze could learn a little by studying gopros setup.All in all, if I can resolve the stream to cloud issue, I will buy more of these.

title: Card slots no good
body: I bought two of these cameras and used them for one full day. I was highly impressed. For the price I paid, I feel like it was a steal!! It was very easy to set up the cameras on the app. You have to know your home Wi-Fi password for your camera to connect to Wi-Fi. It allows you to check the cameras anywhere where you have cell phone service. Also, you can actually turn the sound on and off, and you can speak through it. For instance, I was at a restaurant last night and told my dogs we would be home soon. They heard it very well, clear in the other part of the house because they came running into the living room. You can also take pictures and playback. I am surprised that I paid so little. Definitely worth buying if you want to check on your pets throughout the day and do not want to spend a lot of money. I recommend buying a couple. Side note: The camera does not pan and you have to plug it in to an outlet.However, on the downside, I tried to insert micro sd cards according to the specifications, so yes, I got the type of micro sd card that they say to get. I did not buy the wyze cam cards, I got the Sandisk. Doesn’t work. Not one bit. I tried several times, and the cards do not click in place and click out of place so that you can easily insert or remove it. I had to unscrew the backs to get the cards out. And again, they didn’t work. So 👎🏻👎🏻. I can only see in real time what is happening, and can’t record any of it. I refuse to buy the Wyze cam brand after all of that trouble. It was a five-star review; now it is a 3 star. I can still turn on my app and see what is happening at home in those particular spots, but I can’t rewind.

title: Misses a lot but still nifty
body: UPDATE: For unknown reasons, the settings on the app will change themselves. It will set a time range for recording events, so event recording will be turned off. I've had events disappear without me deleting them. Again, for the price, they are neat little cameras. But I wouldn't rely on them as a reliable security system.I bought this more for fun than for security, thankfully. But I've had it about a week and I notice it misses a lot. We have it set up through a window recording our front walk and driveway. It will catch cars coming and going, but will miss someone walking right in front of the camera. Like when my hubby leaves for work, it doesn't always catch him walking directly in front of the camera to get to his car but it will record an "event" when he backs up to leave. We've tried turning up the sensitivity but then we get every bird and bunny that flitters by. It also doesn't record what it shows on the screen. For example, I got a package delivered and it recorded the event of the car driving in and I got notification while the car was still there. So, I hit the "record" button hoping to catch video of the driver but when I reviewed what was recorded, it was only 4 seconds of the car backing up. I never saw the driver walk past the camera, either coming or leaving. Some of that may be our ISP as we don't have the fastest internet because we live in the sticks but I might try an SD card. Although, I don't know if you get notification if it is continuously recording on the card. It still has a pretty decent picture, especially for the price. I am still thinking of getting a couple more for around the house.

title: Do not scale well
body: I have a total of 12 cameras around the office and inside the assembly equipment. Although they are extremely easy to set up they perform more poorly the more there are. Other than the limitation of only connecting to a 2G wireless network they connect very easily. The streaming rate is pretty poor so HD is not really possible even though there is a setting that makes it appear like a feature. Even the SD & 360p are very choppy, but sharing the cameras with others is super easy.It is pretty amazing that for $25, I can hear and speak through the camera. The app is very underdeveloped so managing more than a couple cameras is clunky. Switching form one camera to the next requires reducing the camera from landscape to portrait, then backing up to the camera list, rotating your device to portrait, scrolling and selecting the next camera then tapping the expand icon in the corner of the video, then rotating your device to landscape. It does take longer than I expected to connect to the camera and start streaming video, which also detracts from having more than a couple cameras.The hack in December exposed the significant security issues with them, so if security concerns you it is probably best to stay away from Wyze.I would suspect the company is making millions from these low cost cameras, I can not wrap my head around why the app is still garbage. Sure hope they figure it out sooner rather than later.A landscape thumbnail page for cameras instead of the longer and longer list that I have to scrollAuto-rotate the video when I rotate my device, seriously how is this not already there?

title: Will not video through a window at night.  The Infrared bounces off the glass.
body: The description makes it seem like it works at night BUT NOT OUT OF A WINDOW!! The infrared camera bounces off the glass at night, but not during the day. Who knew!? I bought it directly from Wyze, but used Amazon pay so they can verify this review is legit.Return policy says that unless it's broken, I can't return it. The only way to get to all the questions about this cam is to search through a long list of questions that I only found once I had an account and learned the camera wasn't "seeing" through the glass at night.I spent 3 hrs per 3 days trying to make this cam work, and it didn't monitor the driveway after dark? I felt cheated. Set-up is a little dicey, too. First time, it worked, but I did it in my livingroom because that's where the laptop is and I used the site as a point of reference. Then I unplugged it (Big Mistake) and brought it to the front bedroom where it would live. I could not get it to work. Would not connect. Contacted Support. 2 days later I get 2 emails. 2 answers. Today, I had time, so I tried again. Saw something in the email that I hadn't noticed before about creating a 2nd device. Ok, that worked. Then to mount it. And to find that when it gets dark, it sees its reflection in the glass and that's all.UPDATE: Wyze initially suggested an IR Blaster and I considered that, but upon further investigation, they said they hadn't had much luck making that work, so they are refunding my money. I was not expecting that. It's true I didn't even know the questions to ask about Infrared/barriers/glass/night, but it's nice of them to do that.

title: Decent hardware crippled by horrendous Android software
body: Everything should be prefaced with a "okay for the price" warning. That said...I'm in the "internet business" and have a corresponding wired and wireless network here.We are experimenting with inexpensive cameras to recommend to people for inexpensive basic security surveillance in their homes. The Wyze seemed to fit the bill despite not having a web interface available.We had some issues connecting the camera to the wi-fi network, but it wasn't anything too difficult. Once we moved the camera further from the access point, it got squirrelly. The Wyze cam was in a position closer to the wireless access point than two D-Link cameras, and it wouldn't maintain a solid connection at first. It isn't terrible now, but I have turned the video quality down to 360p.I installed the Android application on my phone, set up my account, added the camera. None of that was difficult. The app, however, is so poorly written that it appears to freeze when selecting almost ANY function. It's essentially unusable in it's current form as of March 21, 2019. My advice is to avoid this camera if you use Android OR be prepared to return the equipment if the software isn't corrected by the time you try it.I tried the iOS app on an iPad and the difference is notable. The iPad app isn't great, but it certainly is an acceptable experience given the wealth of recording options and the price of the clip storage and hardware. If you are a strictly Apple person, this could be a good thing for you.We don't have an Amazon device with a screen, so couldn't try the integration with "Alexa" or Amazon TV.

title: Cute camera. Long load time. Not for heavy use.
body: I ordered those because they came highly recommended. They are a great design, easy to set up, the app is pretty straight forward as well. However, my connection is not great. I have awesome WiFi, I pay extra so that my teenagers can play their games and my husband can watch his TV and all my younger kiddos can play and watch their tablets. I have a ring doorbell that streams flawlessly, and a solar powered camera outside that has no connection issues. However, when I open this app I have to click on the room, click on the screen and wait for it to load. After awhile it just stops streaming and it very lagging. I do not feel super comfortable with these cameras as a way to monitor my kids while there is a sitter in the house. After reading another review about getting the motion sensors saying they were not setting the camera on to record (dispute attempting to trouble shoot) I have decided to not move forward with other Wyze products.Like I said, these cameras came highly recommended, but as some one else reviewed, they are NOT a dependable security method. I use them to check up on my kids from time to time. But I feel like I would just go ahead and spend the money on a beefed up security option so I am not using three to five different apps for different security devices that can connect with Alexa or google, or wasting to much of my time waiting for things to boot up.Cute camera. Easy set up. Decent picture when working properly. I really wanted to love it. I give it four stars for an “affordable camera”. 2 stars for “security camera”

title: Great Cheap Camera, But Not the Best
body: Pros:1. Affordable alternative to expensive IP cameras2. Clear picture quality, even in SD3. Wide angle view; no remote panning required4. One or Two-way audio options5. Multiple-angle configuration6. Real-time, live feed7. Will accept non-Fat32 formatted 64 GB micro-SD cards, not just the 32 GB7. Did I mention how affordable this is?!Cons:1. Constantly disconnects from home network and smartphone2. Only 12 seconds worth of recording at a time, not 10-15 seconds, just 12 only3. Only one admin account; others only get shared/view-only option4. Relies on 2.4 gigahertz, not fully supported on 5 gigahertz home WiFi5. Built for indoor use only6. See numbers 1 and 2, very annoying >:/Bottom line:I would recommend this as a cheap alternative to the more expensive IP cameras out in the market. If you're willing to put up with the sporadic disconnection and setting up the app on your phone again, then this is definitely for you. For the money it is definitely worth it, but I would not rely on this as your primary home security system camera, because you either get way too many notifications or not enough. Again, the 12-second recordings are just way too short to get a complete picture of how events end.The best use of this camera is to view your dogs and other pets at home or to keep tab on visitors inside the house. Again, not recommended for security use, which Wyze never claimed it was for.

title: Set up took days.
body: Bought from Wyze directly January 17 (when they had inventory) Shipped January 18th. Arrived January 23rd. Set up attempt failures Jan 25th & 26th Contacted support January 27th. No response since. I guess it's going back.For context I used to run 3 x Foscams, then moved to 3 x Dahuas POE fixed dome hanging off a Synology NAS Surveillance Station. These were to have replaced 2x Flir Secure Cams. I have a google wifi router, 150 down / 15 up ISP. So I'm not a complete newb. However set up can't complete after it scans the QR code. And the problem with problems is then you start googling. So this camera is a Xiaomi. And some people are flashing them due to curious web services used. Your mileage may vary.**** Feb 4 Update ***I finally received an email from support which didn't suggest anything different from the FAQ nor support pages. I figured one more attempt at set up. No luck first time . Another attempt got distracted so I accidentally did an overnight power cycle. Next morning I was shocked with set up completed. Firmware upgraded. Now it works so I've adjusted to 3 stars. It will go to 4 stars if it's reliable.So far: Image is pretty good, compares favourably with much pricier Flir. No disconnects. Alerts doesn't let you define more than between hours and doesn't distinguish between week days + weekend. Motion doesn't let you define zones. Motion doesn't distinguish between people, pets and the ever annoying shadows.

title: Not ready for prime time
body: I bought 2 of these wi-fi cameras directly from Wyze back before Christmas, and have been struggling with them since. Some days one camera will connect to my I-Pad and the other will not. Next day things are reversed; other camera connects and first one will not. Very unreliable. Certainly not for security, and maybe not even for baby monitoring (can't see live view if you can't connect to camera). Pretty sure the problem is not my wi-fi. Wyze recently updated the App for I-pad, and now things are even worse. When I am able to connect with either camera, I only get updated video evey 3 seconds rather than continuous like live view is supposed to do. Then after a few minutes the "live view" freezes and stops working.I do like the micro-SD card in camera, so it can record even if wi-fi and/or internet is lost. Only wish the App would allow me to view it later.Update:Just bought my 4th Wyze cam, and changed review from 2 to 3 stars. Product slowly getting better, but still has some issues. For the price, it can't be beat, but still not suitable for serious security monitoring. The V2 model is better than the older V1, but good luck trying to view recorded video on microSD card thru the camera and App. Resolution is very good, but shutter speed too slow. When a person walks past at normal pace, face is blurred because of slow shutter speed, so police have no way to identify ... unless bad guy stops and looks at camera for a second or two.

title: They charge you for person detect now
body: Don’t get me wrong I love these cameras. I have them in my house to watch my dogs and see who peed on the floor after just letting them out. I also snoop on others....I’m terrible. The cameras are in sight don’t worry and in the living room so I’m not watching anyone inappropriately. Just watching them leave their trash on the couch or seeing who’s been eating my candy. The only issue I have with these cameras are the person detector. When I bought these cameras that was a free feature. Then they took that away from me after having it for four months. Now you have to pay. Money grabbing company is what they are. Another issue is it does not pick up everything. I can stand right infront of the camera and move my arms and it won’t catch it. It picks and chooses. Or when I’m on live video and I’m listening to someone talk it cuts out every 5 seconds. I’ll try to talk to someone on the camera to see if we need anything and I won’t hear half of what they say. My last complaint is the pop ups on the app to submit a video to the company to see if the cameras are working well, but I never want to because it’s a video of my own home. It’s not like they check up on you when you report a video to them because I have accidentally done that on at least two occasions. It’s annoying. Honestly I would stay away from this camera if you are wanting person detect. That’s the reason I bought this camera in the first place, but other than that you get what you paid for.

title: Firmware fail results in 4 hour setup
body: Bought this camera as a gift for my 60+ year old parents so they could keep an eye on their place while they're gone. Coworkers recommended these as a great starting point so I bought one to start to make sure it functioned we'll before committing to more.Everything started off great with a super simple setup. Upon getting setup my dad was prompted in the app to update the firmware. With a click of the button he was good to go... nice, right?! Nope! The updated firmware effective killed triggered event recordings, cloud recordings, and sd card recordings. The only thing that seemed to work was the live stream. So, after 3 hours of reformatting the wyze SD card and troubleshooting their network I stumbled across a post on the community board stating the most recent firmware was causing issues for the user that we're similar to mine. So, after another 30 minutes of manually flashing the previous firmware to the camera, everything worked perfectly. I find it disappointing that such an unstable firmware was released and that what seemed like a super easy setup turned into a 4 hour troubleshooting nightmare. I use a much more expensive camera setup from a competitor on my house and was considering switching over since I could add more cameras on the cheap. After this experience I'm not sure that switch will be happening.Ratings:Physical hardware quality: 4/5Firmware: 1/5iOS/Android App functionality: 4/5

title: Great camera when it works
body: I have 3 identical wyze cameras. I and other persons invited to view them have had intermittent success getting them to connect. One camera reliably connects to the House WiFi, one connects most of the time, and one connects rarely (it works after unplug and plug reboot). All three are not very far from the WiFi router. As I am normally hundreds of miles from the house that we are monitoring, the unplug/replug power reboot suggested by Wyze is not an option for people using them to monitor cabins, second homes, or primary homes when away for extended periods.I can find no apparent cause (WiFi interference, router, etc.) on the user end. I have used the most reliable camera in two different houses with different routers, cable providers, etc with no difference in performance.Assuming the root problem is not a quality control problem (i.e. some cameras are “lemons”), I am hoping Wyze will correct the problems through software upgrades including ability for users to remote command reboot or to set the cameras to reboot on a regular schedule.Otherwise the cameras provide good video (including night vision), zoom, and audio, were easy to set up, and have good features. User can control the features via the wyze app (if the camera is connected).When they connect, these cameras are great and an excellent value. My 3 of 5 overall score is only because of the connection issues.

title: Almost perfect
body: If I were single, I would give it a 5 star rating, however there are a few flaws that need to be addressed via software updates.Pros:- Very good picture/sound recording/streaming quality- Ability to have local storage via micro sd card and recordings will automatically delete once the local storage is full- Can view the live stream on an echo show- Easy app set-up and wall-mounting optionsCons:- When sharing the cam, the other user cannot view past timelines-- they can only view live stream and recorded events. This is bad for needing to see what happened at a certain time of day that wasn't triggered by sound or motion. The only fix for this currently is one login for multiple users. NOT IDEAL.- Can schedule events to be recorded between time duration, but cannot pick which days it applies to-- it just does it everyday.- For the Android app, it appears that the Motion/Sound Sensitivity settings revert back to 50% no matter how many times you change it. I've submitted help tickets to Wyze and no responses. My wife's Apple phone however made the change to desired sensitivity and the change has seemed to have stuck....TBDConclusionGreat camera for price. But if it weren't for the integration with the echo show and the magnetic base for wall-mounting, I would've stuck with the YI camera as it addresses those cons.

title: Unbeatable price, but not so good as it looks.
body: The build quality of this device is fantastic and the price is unbeatable, but I've had two major issues so far after several weeks of continuous use:1. The detection sensor doesn't work so fine as it should and it advertised. I've adjusted the sensibility several times and still doesn't catch some major movements in front of it. Hard to believe but it happens to me. How do I know?? Well, I own two cameras, one of them pointing out towards my garage exit. I leave to and come back from work everyday at the same hours. When I review the events from the camera it only has recorded some of the days, not all. I don't know what else should I do, but is hard to believe that a car moving right in front of the camera is not recorded as an movement event. Don't know what to do about it.2. At least half of the times I try to connect to the cameras to see them "live" from my phone app the connection doesn't work. I'm not able to see them live 50% of the times I try to!! I don't know why, because the camera is working as it is recording events because when I'm able to review them, but the "live" connection to them doesn't work as it should.I believe these are issue that can be corrected via software update, because to me they don't look to be a hardware issue, so hope to see those updates to improve the use of the cameras.Everything else is great for this price.

title: Wyze Cam 1080p HD
body: +1 - Item arrived on time, and not damaged+1 - Item is as described+1 - Item works as intended - mostlyThoughts: I needed some cams that were cheap, and set up quickly for an immediate requirement. These fit the bill, though I had never heard of them at the time. I dropped them on the DMZ, connected my device to the DMZ, set up the app and all was well. I liked it, used it for a couple of weeks and even recommended them to some friends. After a month, however, they became unstable and inaccessible through the app. So I installed TinyCam and everything was good - except for 2 way audio doesn't work through TinyCam. Over time, they did updates, patched the App, things got better in the App and usable again. Only thing I don't like about the App is the "thumbnail" view is not live - like it is in TinyCam. You have to go into each Cam to see live, which then you can't see what's going on in the other cams. For now I use TinyCam to monitor the feeds live, then if I need to talk on a cam I jump into the Wyze App and talk. Monitoring/Alerting is also good. Works pretty well. You might have to adjust the sensitivity as mine was picking up sun movement and shadows casted due to that. Overall, for the money, it's a lot of performance in a small package that only needs a power source. One other thing, these will NOT work on a 5Ghz band, only the 2.4Ghz band.

title: Grossly overrated system
body: Wyze's huge limitations make its cameras less than a great deal.Setup was easy, but beyond that these cameras aren't impressive.A big problem - shared with many such cameras - is that Wyze cams only work with an app. No app, no camera use. I got it to work with a couple of third-part apps such as TinyCamPro, but it's still necessary to log into your account and pass all your data through Wyze to see any video. I had hoped to use some obsolete Android tablets as dedicated Wyze cam monitors, but the delay is too huge. Twenty-two seconds pass before I see motion on the old tablets. With newer Android devices, Wyze lag is less than three seconds. However, with a Victure IP camera, there is very little lag with the old tablets (I did not try the experimental firmware Wyze offers that allows some other kind of connectivity).The Wyze video frame rate is awful. It appears to be around ten frames per second. It looks more like a slide show than video. Image quality is okay.None of these WiFi cameras in my experience are as dependable as wired cameras with security DVRs, offered from many sellers here at Amazon. They record to a hard drive and they're technically rock sold - but of course you have to run wires EVERYWHERE. However, per camera, the price isn't that much higher than Wyze cams and similar WiFi models.

title: A great camera
body: I had originally reviewed this camera a couple of months ago with 5 stars, but given the recent & repeated problems connecting to one of my cameras and issues with Wyze Cam 'servers' I am dropping to 4 stars. Maybe Wyze has been cranking out so many new products lately, that they can not keep up with the support of infrastructure and/or existing hardware. Whatever it is, reliability needs to improve. My original review follows...I bought 2 of the Wyze Cams in February 2019 to supplement my 4 Arlo cameras that I have owned for several years. Here are some of the things that I like about the Wyze Cam… First the price, which is ‘wow’ this is a fantastic camera for $25! To add just 1 Arlo to my existing 4 cameras would set me back $150, or the same price as 6 Wyze Cams. The packaging is well done and minimalistic. Setting up the camera was 4 easy steps that took about 5 minutes, very simple. The camera articulates nicely on its base so that I can mount the base, which is also magnetic, and get the camera pointing in the desired direction. The app works well on my iPhone with no issues. The wifi range seems adequate and compares well to the Arlo. You can shut off the IR light through the software, which helps when looking out a window at night to eliminate reflection. Motion detection and notification seems to work better than my Arlo cameras. My Arlos frequently trigger recording after an object has moved past the frame, completely missing the action, and I have yet to see the Wyze Cam do the same. Picture quality is excellent and with a SD card they store several days of continuous video. The software/firmware stability is so-far flawless and surpasses my experience with several painful Arlo updates and software flaws in the early days. Construction looks and feels solid, however the Arlos win on construction as they are weather proof and they have survived several years of outdoor use for me.Here are some things that would improve the Wyze Cam.First and foremost I would like to see an application for my iMac that would allow me to see my cameras in the same way that I can see them with the iPhone app. Actually, a web based app would allow access anywhere, but raises some security concerns.A more revealing and complete explanation of some of the features of this camera would be appreciated. Wyze has a q&a section on their web page that does a pretty good job, but not everything is covered there. One question that I have not found an answer too is what, how, and where the images are stored. It’s not immediately clear what gets stored on the SD card and what goes to the internet. Although internet connectivity and storage is a concern, I also believe internet storage is a benefit, because a burglar could steal the camera and you will still have the video of the theft when stored remotely. Arlos store everything remotely as well, but provide a bit more control over remote storage.The power cord is bulky for such a tiny camera. I will be looking for different power supply options including battery and solar. Also a USB Light Bulb LampCharger like the one by NYCE Power also might come in handy for powering this camera.An outdoor version of this camera would be nice. I may try a DIY project to make it weather resistant as others have, because at $25 a camera why not? The Reolink Argus Pro w/ solar is another good out of the box option, but at a higher price.Improve the playback and editing of continuous video by adding a fast forward, video cutting and storage transfer. The software slider works well, but better tools for playback are needed.New buyers, maker sure you purchase a fast 16 or 32 gb SD card with this camera.Besides proven durability, the only major thing that Arlo has over the Wyze is outdoor use and 100% completely wireless operation, although the Arlo batteries are a bit of a hassle and expensive. I could buy a couple of Wyze Cams for what I pay in batteries for my Arlos each year. The bottom line is that the Wyze Cam an incredibly good investment.

title: Not a security camera, but well worth the money
body: My rating is a compromise between value for money (hands down a 5+) and its usability (a three). First and foremost, this is NOT a security camera. In fact, Wyze states this clearly in their terms of service: “The products and services are not intended for use as security or life safety devices or services (such as a burglar alarm or monitoring system). Please do not rely on the products and services for any such critical uses.” So that said, adjust your expectations to conform.But for the price, it’s a really nifty monitoring system for non-critical applications, and a lot of thought and design have gone into the product (again, considering the price). Given that you have the correct wi-fi system, the setup is easy and straightforward. The camera is as described with good color and reasonable resolution. On a smart phone, the image looks crystal clear. However, on closer zoom examination, the transmitted image is noticeably degraded with compression artifacts, and thus the ability to recognize faces, license numbers, and the like will be somewhat limited to subjects within 10 or 15 feet of the camera. The night vision works well (black and white), and the built-in infrared lights nicely illuminate a typical room (of course, getting darker with distance). Note that you can’t point the camera through a window at night and use the built-in infrared lights – they reflect horribly on the glass. But the app allows you to turn them off. The microphone and speaker provide intelligible audio within a reasonably sized room. It’s not high quality, but it’s usable.The free cloud file service is a good idea, but for any reasonable use, you will want to get the optional on-board micro-SD card. The cloud storage drawback becomes very apparent due to the 12-second limit on uploaded files coupled with what they call the five-minute cooling off period between uploads. Thus, when some event triggers an upload, such as a person breaking in to your house, the recording ends before the real event occurs (such as that person rifling through all your belongings). I suspect the ‘cooling off period’ is really just a euphemism for data throttling to keep on-line storage to a manageable size. But with the on-board micro-SD card, that problem is solved – it’ll keep recording as long as it sees something happening. (But pay attention to the specs for the card – I bought the Wyze card just to be safe since they guarantee it.)The camera is also capable of time lapse video via an easily configurable app interface. However, I find that it’s buggy and unreliable since the resulting videos are often not accessible via the app. I’ve seen other on-line complaints about this, and the most common fix is to reformat the SD card. Unfortunately, that fix isn’t permanent (in my experience), and the problem isn’t apparent until after you’ve spent hours or days hoping to capture some one-of-a-kind event.The camera is designed to be used indoors. Some users, me included, use it outdoors. I purchased a tight-fitting enclosure to mount up under an eave, which ought to protect them from direct rain and snow, but I don’t have the expectation that they will last for years in that configuration.I had one encounter with their support team. I submitted a request to their on-line customer service system, and a knowledgeable and friendly tech responded within a day. The whole issue was settled with a couple of guided troubleshooting exchanges, and I received a new camera in less than a week from the first contact.In summary, it’s a very cost-effective and useful system for non-critical applications. The system and app seem to be a work in progress, and occasional problems elicit the wrath of users, but the company is generally responsive to problems. So for watching the dogs at home, documenting a storm, etc., it’s a good product. The Wyze development team has done a good job given the cost limitations, and I have subsequently bought three more.

title: Wyze Cam New Customer Review
body: I bought a total of 4 Wyze camera’s 3 outdoor housings ,15 to 25 ft cables and 4- 64-bit SD cards. The Wyze cameras came highly recommended by a co-worker. My co-worker bought these camera’s after a truck was stolen from his driveway and I decided to get them after a homeless man ransacked my neighbor’s house looking for cash while my neighbor was on vacation. In addition to the cameras I bought Cameras in use and beware of dog signs. I am so glad I bought these cameras. I didn’t catch any criminals however; I did catch three rats that were scratching around in my attic. I am so glad I did because, they had chewed on a main high voltage electrical wire that could have caused a fire.Pros:Wyze app is easy to use and cameras are very easy to set up.For the price you do get a lot of bang for your buck.The camera’s and app offer a lot of features. Zoom in zoom out, night vision, event recording, continuous recording, turn cameras on and off, set a schedule for the events to record ex: 7am to 4pm. Events can record when motion or sound is detected. You can hear and talk through the camera and the sound quality is good. There is a SD card playback feature too. The camera is on a movable stand so you can adjust it.The SD Cards record a few days’ worth of continuous footage.WYZE records events and stores any events that you record.The picture quality is really good and gives you a wide view. The night vision is pretty good.Can be used outside if protected.You can connect camera to camera.Works great inside the home where power sources are close by and the WIFI is really strong.There are you tube videos that you can watch that show you how to use, set up and install the cameras.Great for keeping an eye on your pets.Wyze has a lot of tech support, you tube videos, customer service support and a support community.Cons:The outdoor housing isn’t the greatest and you need to install the camera where it won’t get rained on. Example: Under the eaves. Cant’s wait for Wyze to make an actual outdoor camera.Need to have really good WIFI signal. I had to move the Wi-Fi to the center of the house so the cameras I placed outside could catch a signal.I installed a camera on my front and back porches and had to run the cable under the window in between the screen and window jam and up the outside wall for now until I can install out door outlets. So, there is visible wiring.Overall: For a quick fast security camera set up especially, inside the home Wyze is great for the price. Like I said a lot of bang for the buck. Peace of mind while your away from your home.Some of the lower star reviews expressed privacy concerns. There is a camera on and off setting as well as a schedule you can set for when you want the app to record events. I have not had a problem with the cameras continuing to record once I turn them off. even if they did continue to record it would just be on the SD card. I don't have the cameras connected to i cloud and the Wyze app will only record brief events when motion is detected if events is turned on. I have set the outdoor cameras to stop recording events at a certain time and I have not seen any events being recorded outside of the schedule that I set. Also, once I turn the cameras off, they stay off. I have not had them turn back on.Also, these cameras are not maintenance free sometimes there are connectivity issues I think due to sometimes happening with the Wi-Fi connection. Sometimes I get a camera not connected needs to be reboot error message. Sometimes if I just try again later, I can get the cameras to reconnect from the app other times I have to unplug and plug the camera back in and then the cameras work fine. I just got the cameras so I have yet to really look into the problem through the support page.

title: Update, Good, cheap but who is watching us
body: Update: I have been using the Wyze camera for over a month now. It has definite advantages over Arlo and Blink. First off, I love the convenience of plugging in the camera to the outlet. Next you can set notifications for motion and/or sounds. I wish there was a user-friendly way to set notification alert on instead of going into each camera and clicking three or four buttons to turn it on. There is a shortcut function but I was not able to figure it out. I was just alerted by the sound of a knock on the door where there was no motion. My outdoor Arlo cameras did not alert me and did not pick up the motion of someone walking up the steps and knocking on the door. You can also talk through the camera which is not an option on the Arlo or Blink. If only they made a Wyze Camera for outdoor use. I tried pointing it out the window but it doesn’t always pick up motion outside the window. Also for the price it cannot be beat.I have Arlo, blink and Wyze cameras so here’s the comparison. All three systems allow for motion notifications and save recorded clips. I am concerned that Wyze camera is the most hackable video of the three. I would not put it in places where you expect privacy.Arlo we bought an earlier generation that runs on batteries. The batteries are real hassle because you have to keep changing them every few months and more often if there is a lot of camera usage. We placed the cameras out of reach so they can’t be easily grabbed and that makes it harder to change the batteries. The picture quality is not good enough to make out someone’s face from 10 to 15 feet away. It would basically have to be at arms length to be able to identify the person in the video. The motion sensing needs improvement. It often senses movement off camera that we can’t see. We tried decreasing the sensitivity and then it doesn’t pick up until after the person is out of camera view. Also the placement is important since it it takes motion that crosses the camera path. If you put it in a pathway where someone will be walking towards the camera it does not pick it up right away. We have this on a rental property and when the camera goes off-line you have to be on site to reset it. On the plus side, the Arlo app is very user-friendly, has a zoom feature and if you want it for outdoor-use it’s very hardy. I’m glad to see that they put in a rechargeable battery and you can also plug in a wire now so you don’t have to worry about the battery running down.I just bought Blink cameras for indoor use to keep an eye on my elderly parents. The resolution is good and the motion sensor is pretty accurate. These also run on batteries.I got Wyze cameras to use inside my own house to keep an eye on things while we are away. So far this is the cheapest option and just as good for resolution and night vision. The downside is that you need to plug it into an outlet which limits where you can put the camera.I like that on the blink camera a blue light lights up when anyone is viewing through the camera. The downside is that when I’m checking on my mom and she is sleeping, a bright light goes on in the room. But you know if someone is watching you. The view-ee does not know that they are being viewed with the Wyze camera. It is difficult for the view-ee to know when being recorded on the Arlo camera. I wish there was a camera that had better resolution from a distance.

title: Has to be on same network as Show devices
body: I read online that for some devices once it's enabled in Alexa the devices don't have to be on the same network. I decided to try that out with the Wyze cam. It absolutely does not work that way!I ended up removing the camera from Wyze and reinstalling after I put in a repeater to help with the signal. The Shows show good image quality but I will have to get a router to use as an access point because the plug in repeater isn't strong enough. I made the mistake of unplugging the repeater and moving it hoping to get a stronger signal and now the Wyze cams won't show on the Show, but I hear sound - the wind blowing. The video shows okay on my phone app but not on the Show now.Voice relay from the phone to the camera is terrible - a friend of mine who has Wyze cams says the same thing and her router is close to her cameras. So that can't be blamed on a poor/weak signal. The other way around is much better. No buffering whatsoever. Now if only we could use voice from the Show.I discovered that you can do a partial sort of zoom in on the Show, look for four arrows in a circle on the left side of the screen. Pressing on it will zoom in, another press will return to original image.DelayOne of the reasons why I still have analog cameras and use a wired connection for the ip cameras that I have is because of a delay in video and audio with wireless cameras which can get quite bad if the signal is weak or the network is congested. Right now my smart devices are on one network and also because the Wyze cam is far from the router, I get about a 5 second delay.For Google Nest HubSetup was fairly easy and image was sharp. My Nest hub is on a different network and there was no problem connecting.Switch over to IRThis occurs a lot earlier than expected. My regular cams would still show as color, but this camera switches over long before dusk. I have it on a balcony facing south so there is a fair amount of sun even in the late afternoon. Analog cameras with less sun exposure still show color.I have an analog camera focused on the gate and that is about 20 or more feet from the Wyze cam location, the gate is about another 80 feet away from the analog camera, and there is also a light at the gate so I will see how the night vision does when it gets fully dark.Night Vision at nightNight vision is very good at over 90 feet away, most likely because of the various lights in the foreground - streetlight, analog camera night vision. One peculiarity with the app, I had night vision set to auto then clicked the night vision button below it on which then auto changed it to IR so image was in black and white even in the day. I noticed it because I checked it in the day and saw it was black and white. Going back into the app I discovered that it had changed and when I clicked back on Auto it went back to color in the day.Wide angle viewThe camera is advertised as being a 2.8mm lens. This is true, I have an analog varifocal cam installed near the same location that is set at 2.8 mm and the field of vision is much the same.

title: Almost a perfect security camera
body: Wyze has really delivered a great camera for an extremely affordable price with the Wyze Cam. The cameras only cost $19.99 direct from Wyze but you will of course have to pay ground shipping so it makes sense as a Prime member to just eat the slightly extra cost and buy here on Amazon. These cameras are small and easy to mount with the included magnet. The hardest part of the setup process was deciding exactly where to mount them to be quite honest. One important thing to note is that they do not support 90 degree image rotation, only 180. Please save yourself a headache and do not mount your magnet sideways before you even check out the options in the app. There is part of the initial setup where you will need to scan a QR code displayed on your phone screen using the Wyze Cam. Be sure to take the protective film off of the lens first, else it will struggle with focusing on it.Once everything is correctly configured, the streams are quite responsive on the mobile app and load up within 1 or 2 seconds, at most. Going back to look at recorded video on the optional SD card is also very snappy and it's easy to export clips to your device's internal storage. I recommend Android users to use Google Photos to auto sync any clips you record so that they can be later accessed on any device from a browser.I was excited to find that there is now a Google Home integration supporting casting of Wyze Cam streams to any Google Cast enabled device such as a Chromecast, Android TV or Home Hub using voice control. Sometimes I have found that it needs to buffer when you first load the stream but it smooths out after ~10 seconds. I don't know if this is on Google's end or Wyze's but it would be nice to see both of them work on an improvement here. This integration was actually the primary reason I chose these cameras as well as their cost. I think the price point on the Nest Cam, for example, is ridiculously marked up in comparison.I give the product 4 stars overall because of two issues. The first is the aforementioned lack of 90 degree image rotation which should be relatively simple to add in a firmware update. This would really be a minimum effort solution to expand the options that people have when mounting. The second is the fact that the camera is always recording audio even if you have set your recordings to mute audio and turned off the fire and CO alarm alerts. This is not only a privacy issue but also quite annoying if you cast the stream to a device, because it will play audio by default. If the camera is within earshot and the stream plays any sound at all, guess what happens? The camera's always-on microphone picks up the sound from the stream and there's an annoying feedback loop until you either close the stream or manually mute the display that is playing it. Again, this is something that could be corrected with firmware changes and an app update. I know Wyze has a good reputation for listening to customer feedback, so if any company reps read this then please take a look at fixing these issues if they are not already being worked on. I think they are easily the biggest things holding these devices back.

title: Good value for the price
body: Update: new firmware seems to have improved the person AI even with cam on its side. The app has also been updated and a glitch I found was cheerfully figured out by customer support via their Facebook community. The app itself does have a vertical viewing option but when it goes to full screen, the normally landscape view becomes a welcome portrait view for my side mounted cam. I also made a matte black paper covering and backdrop for the cam which pretty much eliminates reflections from the window glass. With illumination from the overhead sensor light at night, the night vision mode works very well without the need for the IR lights. In HD video quality is excellent. In SD it’s fine for phone screen viewing but less desirable for larger screens. I pretty much use HD all the time without any bandwidth issues. I’ve also been able select a smaller detection to just pick up motion from the edge of the walkway to the front door that has helped cut down on random motion notifications. I also have one in my living room over looking the back patio in its normal landscape orientation and it definitely does a better job of using the AI to detect people. Far more hits, very few misses and almost no false positives. Feisty, the wyze forums and Facebook group have been a great resource and place to post ideas for new features or discuss new product ideas.Original:Fairly happy with cam. I put it in side window next to the door to watch for package pirates and people who may be coming to the door. Existing doorbell is a poor location for a cam and no other decent location for mounting that is accessible to power. To get a proper view, I had to turn cam on side to get enough vertical viewing area to capture people. Only irritation is being on it’s side the people recognition AI is nearly useless. Meanwhile even with sensitivity at 1(vs100 for max) we still get dozens of motion events for tree branches or shadows moving in the breeze or cars passing on the street. Other irritation is putting cam in side for a vertical view means it displays sideways in landscape format. NoOption in the app to view in portrait mode. However, switching view to full screen and holding phone in portrait orientation will allow viewing the video properly. That said, for $20 it’s hard to find anything anywhere near as good a video quality. The app generally works well. Pretty much everything is done via the app once it is setup - basically just one button on cam to connect to WiFi and begin setup. Option to view events saved as 12 sec clips on cloud server is free with up to 14 days storage which auto rolls over and it will record events or continuously to a micro sd card. Can view sd playback via app or put in reader on computer. No remote computer access. No option to view via computer or browser at this point. Night vision IR illumination is a bit weak for outdoors and like all these it doesn’t work through windows due to the reflection. None of these type cam’s work well through windows at night due to glare and reflections. But I’ve seen indoor video at night indoors that looks pretty good. It’s a young company and so far support has been great.

title: Excellent Optics, Problems with Connectivity/App
body: Quick: Its a decent camera for a nice price, but be prepared to purchase a few accessories and deal with some issues.Pros: + Its got a nice 1080p 30fps(ish) camera with a 110 Lens + It looks nice. + Its compact. + The system recognizes the difference between a person and a squirrel, so you're not getting notified when say, a spider crawls across the lens.Cons: - You'll end up throwing away everything else in the box besides the charger (you'll need a longer cord) - It doesn't have a mount screwhole like other cameras do, which means if you want it to be solid, you got to buy a housing with a mount for it. - You'll also want to buy a 128gb SD Card (per cam) for continuous record. If you don't pony up for an SD Card, you'll miss anything that happens. The 12s record function does not work under any situation. - Sometimes the cameras just don't sync to the servers. They still record continuously, but you'll need to reboot them manually. It doesn't usually happen often, and you usually can access the camera by still tapping on it (for some reason), but the system doesn't notify you of issues, and those 12s clips don't get recorded.I purchased this camera because it was meant to replace the expensive Nest Cam that I purchased a while back as part of a package deal with the Nest Protect. However, I decided to keep both the Nest Cam (on free mode), and also this cam for each takes care of its shortcomings. Nest Cam connects with Nest Protect which means I get a neat little video if the Nest Protect ever goes off. The Wyze Cam gives me the continuous recording with the 128gb SD Card I installed in it. I installed the SD Card 15 days ago, and it still has 40 gb of storage remaining on the chip, putting it right about 20 days of storage, for only a one-time purchase of $15 on Amazon.That being said, the fact that they provide a magnetic mount made me laugh. Its not secure by any means, and that also means someone can just swat it down in a quarter of a second. Instead, you're going to want to buy a mount (got a decent one on Amazon: $10) that has a wall-screw mount. There's a few options, and I bought one which is also waterproof because it has a secure housing. You'll also want to drop for a long enough cord. You get four feet of cord, which means that either the camera is just above eye-level, or you're bucking for a USB Cord extender. I opted for a 25' USB Cable ($8 on Amazon). I drilled a hole through the ceiling in my foyer to get to a power box in the attic, and it was just fine.The app is nicely done, but it has its flaws. Connectivity on the home screen is pretty spotty sometimes, requiring a manual reboot of devices (rebooting from the app does not work, even if you can connect to live view). Firmware updates sometime take multiple attempts/manual reboots to take. Notifications happen instantly sometimes, while some other times, they'll vibrate an hour after it occurred. Despite all of this, I find the fact that it's a cheap camera with excellent optics a pretty good buy, if you're willing to deal with a few bumps along the road.

title: The setup is easy, the video quality is solid
body: The Wyze camera has been a learning experience that is well worth the pittance you pay for it. The setup is easy, the video quality is solid, and it has good features. If you buy and install a smartcard, you solve a lot of the problems people have complained about. Let me start with the learning part. Indoor cameras, like this, have limitations if you want to monitor what's going on outside your house, particularly at night. Motion detection is one issue. This camera, as do several far more expensive ones, use some kind of algorithm that creates an alert based on when and how quickly pixels change from frame to frame. This means, for example, that the sudden darkening caused by a cloud obscuring the sun triggers an alert. You can adjust this by decreasing the sensitivity, but the bottom line is that the camera tends to pick up unnecessary alerts. By the same token, a smaller object passing in front of it, such as the mailman, often doesn't trigger an alert. More expensive cameras compensate for this by letting you create a zone, such as the sidewalk up to your house, for the camera to look for motion. That way, it would ignore the trees limbs blowing in the wind in the background. Some cameras use infrared rather than a pixel-change algorithm to detect motion. This technology is better in some ways because only the motion of warm bodies, such as humans and automobile engines, will trigger an alert. Moving trees and cloudy skies won't. As an indoor camera, this also doesn't do well in picking up things outdoors at night. It has an infrared light to light up the scene at night, but infrared reflects off the glass in a window, so it's useless for this purpose. If you have outdoor lighting, the camera will do a fair job. It will at least let you see cars and people passing by. You're not going to recognize license numbers or faces however. For $25 though, this does several things pretty well. For example, I saw traces of mice in my house and set up this camera to see how many there were. The old saying is "where there's one mouse, there are others." About 1 a.m., the camera provided an alert. Although that snippet was only 12 seconds long, I could playback the entire episode which was stored on the smartcard and could see there was only one mouse cavorting around for the ten minutes or so before his demise in the trap. And the camera has alerted me to cars pulling up in front of my house at 2 a.m. And certainly if I had the camera looking at a window or door from inside, it would give me an alert if someone tried to enter. Not bad for $25. My conclusion is that I probably don't need to spend several hundred dollars on a security camera that will do more. Indeed, I have the sense that I'd need to spend a great deal more to get a security system that would work at night outside my house. The Wyze is remarkably good within its limits and has given me an education in security cameras that is well worth what I paid.

title: A decent system for the price!
body: I ordered 2 of these. The first one set up very quickly, and I was happy to have it working in about 30 minutes. Most of that time was setting up the app, and creating an account.The second one kind of turned into a 3-hour ordeal. The thing was, it had a broken set-up button. It took a long time to figure this out, and I would have been even more confused if I didn't have the first one go smoothly and orderly as a point of reference.I opened it up and found that the internal button pillar for the setup button was missing, and black plastic fragments [ of the pillar connection, I assume] were stuck in the connection housing box, preventing the connection plate from disconnecting, which resulted in an endless set-up loop where it would reset with a chime and: "Ready to connect!"I dislodged most of the fragments which let the connection plate go back to its default position, and this let me finally finish the set-up, and start using the camera. With the low price, and being that it was functional after bypassing the defect, I figure it's not worth the hassle of a return, and overall, it won't deter me from buying more--I just got unlucky on that one. It's still a good deal. It is affordable because it's cheaply produced, and cheaply produced means a higher percentage of defects.They do have 4 infrared lights which shine through the black-out plastic in the circle around the camera. I didn't experiment with these much, as my cameras look through glass, and I mostly just see the reflective glare of the 4 IR lights in the shot, but I'm sure they work well to light a room for infrared view when the camera doesn't have to look through glass.A couple of curious things which I learned through disassembly, and also watching disassembly videos, is that there are 2 missing components on these cameras which were located behind the little circles at the top and bottom of the front. The top circle seems to have a spot on the board for an LED light which does not exist. I'm kind of glad for this. I think after initial design, it was decided that a front-facing LED to identify recording kind of killed discretion.The bottom circle's missing component is kind of a mystery. Nothing exists on the board behind it, nor are there any connections to it. Maybe it was originally there for a motion sensor that was deemed redundant if the camera's always processing motion anyway, or maybe it was going to be for an over-budget thermometer? A front-facing speaker? Who knows. It doesn't exist now at any rate.In summary, I learned first-hand that these cameras can come with quirks. However, I also learned that they are a decent system for the low, low price, and I'll probably get more.

title: Best Value for the Money - Solid in all conditions
body: The Wyze Cam may very well be the best security camera system on the market right now. It is supported by an app on your phone that allows you to record, use talk back features and set up your cameras with custom names so you always know what angle you are looking at.I currently have 6 cameras positioned around my house and for an investment under $150 bucks, I feel like I have a $2000 security system installed.The simplicity of these cameras is their best attribute. They are deceptively robust. Mine have been mounted with a magnetic plate outside my windows on my second floor, allowing me to zone my entire home's perimeter with complete camera coverage.Installation is a breeze with magnetic plates and flat USB cable that will adapt to most window frames. I open my window, set the metal plate with the included adhesive ring and then position the camera on it. The cable is run under the window and I close it. The flat wire makes a snug fit, allowing me to even lock my window without damaging the wire. My home has plugs under each window, or nearby and I am able to plug these in without rigging or adding and extension cords.The cameras are somewhat protected under my roof's eave keeping them from getting soaked in a rainstorm, but otherwise, they sit outside in summer and winter and have zero mechanical problems with them. They hold up like nothing I have seen. The microphones are very sensitive and they can pick up frogs in a nearby creek.Annoyingly, in the summertime, the bugs are attracted to the IR and tend to trigger the cameras motion sensor A LOT! It is probably a reality of all types of cameras with IR sensors. This is why I have only given 4 stars in motion detection rating.Picture quality is pretty good during the day. Colors are true and there is good, sharp images that can be zoomed in a bit. Not as good as what a 4k would do, but keep in mind the camera is only $25! So for the money, you will be satisfied with the clarity of the view.The App has gone through some updates of late and I have noticed a bit more lag in its use since then. Not sure why that is, but I have given 4 stars for this reason in the overall rating.Overall, if you want a fast and easy installation, robust cameras that do what they are designed to do, and do it well for an amazing price, look no further!Increase your peace of mind, keep on eye on your property, know when your Amazon deliveries arrive or when those pesky salespeople knock on your door. Wyze has got your covered! I don't recommend many products with enthusiasm. The value and quality here make this a safe bet.

title: I may have found a new hobby....
body: I bought this on a whim after getting a new Echo Plus for Christmas. I have been wanting to try this type of camera but couldn't justify the time or cost. I now have the time, and the price of this camera needed no justification.*Setup*: I followed the included quick start guide which is very well done. During set-up, however, I did not receive the promised audio prompts from the camera. I opted to proceed as instructed, with the assumption that everything else was working, using the indicator lights to determine whether progress was being made--and it was. I was able to complete setup successfully without the voice prompts.Using the app I installed on my iPad, I had no trouble setting up the camera:- Under "Event Recording," I enabled the detection of both sound and motion. This captures a video clip triggered by a sound or motion event and sends you a notification of the event.- I was able to set a detection zone, which I set to the doorway into the room. This way only motion events within that zone will trigger an event. I also elevated the zone a bit so that the cat wasn't triggering motion events.- I set a schedule so that these events would only be triggered during our work hours--in other words, only when we're not home. Options for schedules do not include days of the week -- so I can't exempt Saturday & Sunday. There is also only one schedule setting. In other words I can't ask it to detect events during work hours AND overnight, but not when we are most active in the house. A minor impact to me but it also seems like something it wouldn't be that hard for Wyze to include (but I could be wrong about that!)- I chose to enable detection of sounds from the smoke and CO detectors, which means the camera will be triggered to capture video and send me a push notification if either alarm goes off.Overall, I am very impressed with this camera and can't wait until springtime so I can set a time-lapse video of my container garden. The only negatives are minor ones-- 1) the lack of audio prompts during setup (which didn't prevent me from completing setup) and 2) the limited flexibility in event recording (i.e., more ability to specify when to capture events and notify me).I also enjoyed the clips shared by other users that you can see under the "Discover" section of the app. Gave me lots of ideas. Clips from other users include a cranky starling, quite a few attempted car thefts, nesting doves, baffled squirrels, a time-lapse herb garden, and my fave: Hawk vs. Snake. So many things to see, and Wyze cameras give you an easy, fun, and inexpensive way to watch your world.

title: Good but has some issues to be aware of
body: At first blush, $25 for a cloud enabled camera with no monthly fees and mobile device access seems great, right? Let's start with the pros...* Setup is amazingly easy. In the app you type in your wi-fi SSID and key which creates a QR code on the screen. From there you click the setup button on the camera and point it at the QR code - brilliant.* This thing is small, light, and has a magnet. You can stick it anywhere though you do need a power outlet to plug it into.* The picture is surprisingly good both in light and darkness. It has an integrated IR night mode.* The motion capture is very sensitive. You can adjust this in the settings.* This camera has 2 way audio! That's right, you can talk and listen. It's a little disjointed and the sound quality leaves something to be desired. Still, it's passable and unexpected in such an inexpensive device.Unfortunately there are some gotchas to be aware of:1. You'll need an SD card. You can get a 32GB for $5 which keeps the total cost around $30. The size you need ultimately depends on the amount of motion capture which will be unique for every situation.2. By default everything is stored and played back using the SD card (i.e., if you don't have one, you'll only get live video for the most part). You can enable motion capture triggers which will store 12 second clips in your cloud account for 14 days. Keep in mind it only stores one 12 second clip every few minutes so this is not a viable replacement for the SD card.3. It does not have any battery option and will not record during a power outage.4. You can only view the cameras on an iOS or Android device using their mobile app. You can *not* view the cameras on a PC or via a web browser which may limit use from work and other situations.5. Technical support and customer service have been sub-par in my limited experience. For example, I got locked out of my account after making an account change. All they could tell me was "wait 24 hours and try again" - which didn't work and I had to re-chat with them and they had to escalate, etc etc. All the while I had no access to the cameras.My biggest fear is they can walk off pretty easy. It seems a little too easy to reset these so there may be issues in higher traffic or public areas. I've only had these a few weeks but so far they're pretty impressive for the cost.

title: Wyze is a great "complimentary" camera system for an existing security setting that uses a more robust AI or cloud storage
body: Wyze is a great "complimentary" camera system for an existing security setting that uses a more robust AI or cloud storage offerings like Nest or Arlo.This is particularly true if you're looking to gain multiple angles to a room without breaking the bank. The compact and well balanced design of the camera along with its magnetic base makes it less noticeable allowing it to move closer to the subject.Also for those who still opt for an off-line security system which offers the most robust and private recording settings, Wyze could be the minimal add-on for online viewing.Bugs as of firmware 3.9.1.102:- Connection to the Bluetooth headphones are lost if you're trying to stream audio. Switching to plugged headphones still causes the phone speakers to turn on briefly.- Sometimes the panoramic mode button disappears. Toggling back and forth between HD and SD seems to help it re-appear.- Sound settings are set to mute by default and cannot be remembered like the video definition. In short you have to press the speaker button every time you pick a video. Also sound returns to mute after a time or if the connection is shoddy.Issues with Connection with current App (as of 1/30/2018)- You cannot establish a connection to 5Ghz. Not a major issue except that when the application draws the information from your smartphone to setup a new device and you're on the 5Ghz you may have to manually name the 2.4Ghz network you need the device to connect it to.Design compromises but not deal breakers:- A toggle in the app allows the camera to be rotated 180% which it's great but would be great to be able to see 90 and 270 degrees as well.- The panoramic mode only tilts one way and will not readjust to your phone's gyroscope.- Included micro USB cable is 6 feet long which should be enough for most over the counter or bookshelf placements but not enough to be placed high up for birds-eye-views.- Sound quality is sub-par to muffled noise and conversations cannot be understood unlike the expensive options.- Application is not locked by Touch ID (for iOS) but if privacy was a concern you could just as well keep the phone locked. The Touch ID option in the Arlo app in fact is default and cannot be turned off which I found it more annoying than necessary even from a robust security system.

title: Great price and software
body: These little (pixar-like) Wyze v2 cubes have a wide angle view and a much more functional software than with camera freeware like Alfred. I had been using Alfred on an old phone and tablet and I tried other freeware but Alfred seemed the best for my Android devices. I actually found this camera looking for a second-hand phone to upgrade what I was using with Alfred but I could not buy a reasonable old phone for the price of these camera cubes. Compared to Alfred advantages are that you can set up automated buttons for sleep, awake, I'm away and home on the home page, and also automate on/off by specific times. You can capture still pictures and do continuous monitoring with rewrites to a 32GB (max) SD card. Captured events can be deleted immediately and after trying capture with sound and motion, there are just too many sounds that get captured so I only capture actual motion. I also had problems with the old equipment not being able to restart after a power outage which was a problem when I was traveling but it looks like the Wyze v2 camera can be rebooted remotely. The real test of controlling this Wyze v2 camera will be on a upcoming out of country trip. If this camera continues to perform I'll be getting 1-2 more. My only complaint is that these cameras only work on 2.4 GHz wireless so I had to set up a separate channel on my router as these could not connect with an alternating 2.4/5 GHz router setting. I'm not sure why so many smart devices cannot handle 5 GHz or roving 2.4/5 GHz router settings. I am able to use the app on a phone that is set for roving 2.4/5 wireless, so maybe it is only the setup that requires your phone or tablet to be on 2.4 only wireless setting. I just keep my tablet permanently on the same 2.4 wireless as this camera and for setting up other 2.4 GHz requiring wireless equipment. Update Mar 2019 - deducted a star - I discovered after returning from my trip that Wyze had disconnected. I'm not sure when this happened as I automated settings and I could still see a live view before I left but I did not test the motion. I had to delete and add the camera again to get the camera connected again. Since then it has run fine. Before I leave on another trip I will make sure to test the motion sensor before I leave to make sure that the camera is really doing what I bought it for. I'm holding off on buying another camera until the promised integration with Google Assist is available.

title: 12 second snippets
body: While it is mentioned in the product information that it "automatically records a 12-second Event Video when it detects motion or sounds", what is not mentioned is that this length is not adjustable. Additionally, following the camera recording your 12-second event, it becomes automatically "locked out" from recording anything else for the next 300 seconds (5 minutes). The only workaround for both of these is to record locally on the camera via SD card, however, you then lose event notifications and the ability to have the camera act somewhat like an alarm that lets you know when something is happening. Just to add on to this matter is the fact that since life typically happens in longer than 12 second events, you'll want to be using the continuous recording mode, where you'll potentially have a helluva lot of recorded video to preview through to find anything, as nothing gets "tagged" as it does when in event recording mode.It was also a bit disappointing to discover that there's no way to connect thru a PC, which removes the concept of having an area monitored continuously on a PC screen that you might have running in an office, kitchen, etc or for use as a baby cam or similar.Install and setup was super easy and straightforward; it practically installs itself by using the QR code. Playback is of decent quality, night vision is less than desirable with very noisy gray playback. The cameras thus far have been quite reliable to the detection of both motion and sound, and both are quite sensitive as I keep adjusting mine still searching for the sweet spot where the rustling of some bushes near my front walkway won't trigger an event.Overall, although there are certain shortcomings that; had I known of them in advance, might have influenced my decision to go with something different, it wouldn't be fair to review this camera and not mention the price - because there's nothing I'm aware of that does what these do for the price. Essentially, I got a 2 camera system, also purchasing the outdoor cover and a 25' extension cable for under $70.00, less than what you could expect to pay for a single camera. Having the included, zero subscription cost 100% free 14-day cloud storage makes this an unbeatable deal, even though it's likely the free cloud storage is why we get 12-second event snippets...

title: I really like my Wyze Cams!
body: Let me begin by stating that I had very low expectations for this product. For the price, if it turned on and connected semi-consistently to my phone, I'd considered that a big win. But my experience has been so much better than that. The presentation in the packaging, the ease of setup, and the full-featured, but easy to use app...this camera has really impressed me. The company behind it is also quite interesting. Wyze seems to be keeping an open discussion on product feedback and has added some requested features. We're talking about a camera that costs $25 shipped.I bought my first one because I was pretty annoyed with my Ring Doorbell 2. Obviously they're not selling to the same market, Wyze products are not intended for security use. I just didn't want to spend big money on something better, and Wyze has been getting a lot of praise in the tech media. To my surprise the setup was a breeze, the connectivity is more consistent than my Ring Doorbell 2, the app offers more options, the camera accepts a micro SD card, and the video quality is better. What the #$@!I currently have 3 cameras in use around the house. I haven't had any issues so far. They're not perfect, but it's hard to be too critical over $25 toys that I'm irresponsibly using as security cameras. However, I do find the 12 second upload to the cloud is rarely enough time. I would gladly reduce the storage time from 2 weeks down to 1 week, if the uploaded videos were twice as long. Also, the motion and sound activation is too sensitive. I have one mounted in my garage that was constantly triggered by every drawer or door that was closed inside my home, and every car engine that drove by. Even on the lowest sensitivity it was a problem, so I ended up disabling sound notifications. If you have it mounted outside, or in a garage, the infrared lights tend to attract flying bugs. They will set off the motion sensor repeatedly. Fortunately, the cameras work with IFTTT, so it's pretty straight-forward setting up an applet that will disable push notifications when you're at home.As stated before, it's hard to be too critical considering my original expectations. I'm completely happy with it for the price I paid. If you like to tinker with tech, I think you'd probably enjoy having one (or several) as well.

title: Good little camera, but room for improvement
body: Very happy with this little camera. I only have one (so far) and have it set up to see my living room, dining room and kitchen. It is in the corner of the big room and can easily see down both walls (90 degree corner). I have a Night Owl POE NVR system also, but one of the cameras stopped working and I didn't feel like spending $150 on a replacement camera for a system that I really don't like. So I tried this one.The picture is very clear and night vision is actually better on this 1080P camera than the 4K Night Owl cameras I have, much better actually. The two way audio isn't great, its weird that you can't just turn it on and talk back and forth, but it honestly isn't real important to me.Notifications are NOT quick. I turned off the sound notification but motion notifications are about 10 minutes late all the time. Not a good thing, but it doesn't bother me because I have a security system on the house and this camera in inside, on the top floor. The actual sensor seems to work pretty good. The notification would be a problem for me if the cameras were outside. I won't buy another until this problem is fixed.Pro:PriceSmall sizeClear pictureEase of useFunctionality - App is easy to useCon:Only uses 2.4GHz networkNo battery backup, unless you add your ownWould be nice if the power cord was longer, but you can buy an extension (or get your own)Max 32GB Micro SD card, would be nice if 128GB would be maxShort detection recordsCouple big things in other reviews: The cameras do not transfer video feeds through countries outside of the US any longer. Shame it happened for a while, but was corrected with firmware updates. Also, if this item catches fire and burns your house down, you are not out of pocket to rebuild. That is what insurance is for, your insurance company will go after the cause of the fire - being the power supply for this, gas leak on your stove, water leak on fridge, aliens, neighbor's tree falls on house, etc.Definitely would purchase another, hopefully they add products to their lineup. Just needs some tweaking before I really want to pick up more of them. I also need (or just want to) more places to put them too, so there's that.

title: A nice little camera
body: Ok, for $25 u can’t ask too much out of a little camera like this right. Well outside of that I was very surprised. Was only difficult trying to scan the UPC code. Had to lay it down and then scan. Took me quite a long time to figure that out. This is coming from a guy that can tear apart windows/Mac laptops and repair those problems so why was this such a pain in the ass. Have some other ones that were a lot more expensive but also set up real easy.Ok so this little camera will handle quite a lot and gives u a nice picture and alert. You can’t change directions on this unit by application. Only can be done physically.. For my purpose this little guy is fine. Had a weak entry spot that I wanted a bit more coverage on. House is alarmed very well but visible detection is important. What I found most compelling was the night vision. Completely the best I ve seen. Not that I have tons of info but I was really surprised on how well it views a completely dark area like it was daylight. There are no visible led lights that will alert someone as to this effect which is awesome for me. Wish my more expensive ones could do that. I am talking 90$ plus and they can’t give me this view. I think the app needs work and maybe for these devise to play better with other apps. I would of like to link this to my zwave devises. So it’s not a do all devise but for a little info maybe like for a child’s room and watching what ur dog is doing is all good.The speaker quite frankly sucks. Sorry to say but it’s not rocket science it’s the cost of materials that’s the bottom line. You can’t put a camera out there for 25$ and expect it to be flawless if every way. Won’t happen, something has to give right. So it’s not a top of line camera but it did surprise me and did the job I needed to get done. Good luck with your decision it’s not easy with all that’s out there. There’s wired systems with dvr’s and cost a lot more but that’s another subject.
